title: Observable — Life & Death of a Star
logline: From nebula to main sequence to stellar remnant — how mass sets a star's fate; Hubble and supernova observations cited.

brand.subtitle: Upon Tyne Science — evidence before wonder

beats.speech_text: Every atom of iron in your blood was forged inside a star that lived, burned, and died long before Earth existed.
beats.on_screen: Observable · Life & Death of a Star

beats.speech_text: Stars begin as dense knots in cold gas clouds. Gravity pulls hydrogen together until fusion ignites — a main-sequence star. Mass is destiny: Sun-like stars swell into red giants; the most massive stars race through fusion stages in millions of years.

We read stellar ages and compositions in spectra — the fingerprint of elements made in the core. Hubble and ground telescopes watch star-forming nebulae today. Supernova SN nineteen eighty-seven A showed, in real time, what theory predicted for a collapsing massive star.

This time-compressed model walks one star from nursery to remnant — white dwarf, neutron star, or black hole depending on mass. Billions of years, seconds on screen — a simulation, not a single observed timeline.
beats.visualization_prompt: stellar evolution montage: molecular cloud collapse, main-sequence G-type star, red giant with concentric shells, Type II supernova flash and expanding remnant, labeled mass branches, documentary astrophysics fidelity

beats.speech_text: When massive stars explode, they seed the galaxy with heavy elements — the raw material for planets and life. Stars are not background lights. They are the universe's chemical factories.

lines: SUBJECT: Life & Death of a Star (STAR-LIFE-001)
EVIDENCE: Stellar spectroscopy; SN 1987A; Chandrasekhar limit theory
